topic: Aspects of Political Life in the Hispanic World
subject: spanish

items.q: When did 'la transición' to democracy take place in Spain?
items.correct: After Franco's death in 1975, leading to the democratic Constitution of 1978.
items.misconception: The transición happened straight after the Civil War ended in 1939.
items.random_wrong: The transición took place during the First World War.
items.why: Students compress the timeline: 1939 brought Franco's dictatorship, which lasted thirty-six years; democracy only began to be rebuilt once he died in 1975.

items.q: Does the Spanish king govern the country?
items.correct: No - in a parliamentary monarchy the king reigns as head of state, while the elected government and the Cortes govern.
items.misconception: El rey makes Spain's laws and runs the government.
items.random_wrong: Spain has had no head of state since 1978.
items.why: The phrase 'el rey reina pero no gobierna' captures the constitutional division of roles, a frequent point in essays on Spanish political life.

items.correct: No - it was a grassroots protest movement of 2011; the party Podemos was founded later, in 2014, partly inspired by it.
items.misconception: Los indignados was the name of a party that stood in elections.
items.random_wrong: Los indignados was a Spanish football supporters' club.
items.why: Distinguishing social movements from the parties they inspire shows precise understanding of how popular protest reshaped Spain's party system.

items.q: Franco's dictatorship in Spain ended in which way?
items.misconception: With a military defeat by France
items.why: Correct answer: With his death in 1975. “With a military defeat by France” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Spanish 'indignados' movement (15-M) began in which year?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2011. “1985” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What system of government does Spain have today?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A parliamentary monarchy. “An absolute monarchy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Augusto Pinochet led a dictatorship in which Spanish-speaking country?
items.why: It's Chile. “Mexico” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Franco die, opening the way to Spain's transition to democracy?
items.why: The real figure is 1975. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
